title: WHY LOOKUPS
FAIL.
segments.body_html:
A lookup does one thing: you give it a value, it searches a table, it returns a different value from the same row. Three

Why Lookups Fail — When any of these breaks, you get either a visible error — #N/A, meaning the value was not found — or you get a silent w

beats.text: A lookup does one thing: you give it a value, it searches a table, it returns a different value from the same row. Three things must be true for this to work correctly. The lookup value must exist in the table. The table must be structured the way the function expects. The match mode must be what you actually want. When any of these breaks, you get either a visible error , #N/A, meaning the value was not found , or you get a silent wrong answer.

segments.label: 02 / 10 · WHY LOOKUPS FAIL

Table_array — Table_array controls the table_array decision in this workflow.
Col_index_num — Col_index_num controls the col_index_num decision in this workflow.

beats.text: VLOOKUP searches the leftmost column of a table and returns a value from a column to its right.

title: TEXT IS
NOT NUMBERS.
segments.body_html:
—
CONCAT — CONCAT — key argument to get right applied to the chapter example.
TEXTJOIN — TEXTJOIN — key argument to get right applied to the chapter example.
LEFT/RIGHT/MID — LEFT/RIGHT/MID — key argument to get right applied to the chapter example.

beats.text: A shipping database has customer names stored as "SMITH, JOHN" in all caps. Your report needs "John Smith." A product code is stored as "US-12345-RED." A downstream system needs just the five-digit number. Dates exported from a legacy system arrive as text strings like "03/15/24" rather than as actual date values. These are text problems, and they appear constantly in real data work. The core principle: Excel stores two kinds of things that look identical.

segments.label: 04 / 10 · TEXT IS NOT NUMBERS

title: DATES ARE
NUMBERS.
segments.body_html:
—
TODAY/NOW — TODAY/NOW — key argument applied to the chapter example.
DATE — DATE — key argument applied to the chapter example.
DATEDIF — DATEDIF — key argument applied to the chapter example.

beats.text: Excel stores dates as integers , the number of days since January 1, 1900. The date March 15, 2024 is stored as 45365. When Excel displays it as "3/15/2024," it is applying a format to the underlying number. This matters for date arithmetic: subtracting one date from another gives you the number of days between them, because you are subtracting two integers. If A2 contains a date, this returns the number of days since that date.

segments.label: 05 / 10 · DATES ARE NUMBERS

title: THE VERIFICATION
HABIT.
segments.body_html:
—
Known-Good Input Test — Known-Good Input Test — what to test applied to the chapter example.
Missing/Blank Input Test — Missing/Blank Input Test — what to test applied to the chapter example.
Boundary Condition Test — Boundary Condition Test — what to test applied to the chapter example.

beats.text: Every formula in this chapter can be correct and wrong simultaneously. VLOOKUP with approximate match is correct formula syntax. It produces wrong prices when the table is unsorted. An IFS that checks conditions in the wrong order is correct formula syntax. It misclassifies some records. A TEXT function that formats dates as "mm/dd/yyyy" is correct formula syntax. It produces strings that sort incorrectly. The habit that prevents Friday disasters is verification on edge cases before the formula goes anywhere near real data.

segments.label: 06 / 10 · THE VERIFICATION HABIT

title: EXERCISES
segments.body_html: 1.→2.→3.→4.→5.

beats.text: Write an IF formula for column E that returns "PREMIUM" if column C exceeds $500, and "STANDARD" otherwise. Then rewrite it so the threshold is $500 or above (inclusive). What single character changes? Test both with a value of exactly $500 and explain the difference. Tests: the > vs. >= boundary , the off-by-one that matters in pricing and contracts. Difficulty: low. Column A has a product code in the format "US-12345-RED".

segments.label: 07 / 10 · EXERCISES

beats.text: The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. Grace Hopper was working on the first compiler (A-0, 1952) and FLOW-MATIC , the moves that let humans write instructions in something close to English and have a machine execute them, which is what every Excel formula descends from decades before most people had heard of spreadsheet formulas and functions. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.
